speaker: interviewer
text: Beginning tape number two. Interview with Mr. Harry Schlesinger. It is March the 30th, 1997. Mr. Schlesinger, tell me a little bit now about what happened when the Germans first entered Hungary and Munkacs in particular.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944, when the Germans occupied Hungary, this was approximately a little-- it's before Pesach. Is that correct, [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: March 19.

speaker: interviewee
text: March 19, they entered to Hungary, and right away with a fast speed they started to ship out the Jews to concentration camp. First they took the little places around, the small villages around Munkacs and was so well organized. They had already experience because, the Germans, they did this already since 1939. And they started with the Polish Jews. So by the time they arrived in 1944, they had all the experience, how fast [INAUDIBLE] figured out to ship out all the Jews to concentration camp. In order to do it fast, in order the public shouldn't find out what's going on, where they going, where they sending them, they made a [NON-ENGLISH]. The [NON-ENGLISH] was well-known Jewish people, fine families, most of them rich people. And they told them, this is the situation. They told them the truth, where they sending them, but you are not allowed to tell this to nobody. The public is not allowed to know that they are going to be shipped to a concentration camp, to kill them. And if you close your mouth, or you with your family will be sent to Budapest, and you'll be free from the concentration camp. But, of course, it was a very, very ugly thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: What shall I say if it was right? Of course it was not right. If somebody has a chance to save his family, who knows? Maybe somebody else will do the same thing. I don't give him right. But I can't say if they shouldn't do it. What can I tell you?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out that they were given this offer?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the stories we only find out after the war. They were so well organized. First of all, there was a big brick factory in Munkacs. and this was the place, the central, where the trains came in from the outside, because usually they used to ship with the trains the bricks. So we had to-- first they made a ghetto. The ghetto became two or three times smaller and smaller. And by the end, all the Jews got taken out from the little villages all around the town. Then they took us from the ghetto to this brick factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you had mentioned that the Germans had been deporting Jews from Poland starting in 1939, that they were very well organized. You, living in Hungary before the Germans came in, did you hear anything about what was going on elsewhere in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard a lot. In fact, I with my father used to listen from London the news, BBC. Officially, we couldn't hear it, because everybody had to bring to the city his radio. They was putting a stamp on it, and there was only one station was available Budapest. So you couldn't hear nothing, just the news from Hungary, whatever they were interested we should listen. But came a mechanic, a radio mechanic, and he told me, Schlesinger, to my father, I'll show you how to you could listen it. It's very dangerous. If they catch you, you are next day already in concentration camp. They send you to forced labor. So be very careful before you listen this news. Close up all over the doors, and nobody should know about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he showed us there is a little string in the radio that the button was the way they stamped left there, but we was pulling the string here and there and came evening 9:00, and we heard bum bum bum boom, bum bum bum boom. And this was from London. They did not let us know from London that you go to Auschwitz. They did not let us know from the [NON-ENGLISH] from Israel. They take you to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, we were already in the ghetto, and we were already in the brick factory. There were a few [NON-ENGLISH] from Israel. They took out a few young people to come through Turkey to Israel. But the real truth to tell us, and they made a big mistake. And a lot of people are angry for it, because Munkacs was a place in Carpathian where there are a lot of hills, a lot of hiding places, a lot of rich people who could afford to run away to together. If they would know really the truth of what happened. But they didn't came to save us. They came just to save a few young people to take them to Israel. And it was not nice. This is number one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, let me think what I want to bring you something else out. When they made the ghetto in Munkacs, it became, as I told you before, first was bigger the ghetto, then smaller and smaller. First time when the ghetto became our home, our house, was in the ghetto. When they made it smaller, we had to move already to my grandfather's house, what is two blocks away. And this was my mother's father's house. He was a very, very wealthy rich Jew, who before the war-- first World War-- he had his own bank, and he had such a big house where the ghetto was. But all the Jews were pushed then into this big, big, big house.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my grandfather said to my mother, at least so much I can do for you. There is a little small room, maybe five people could enter in this room. Used to be the washroom when the maid was washing the clothing. We give you this soon. So this way you don't have to sleep with other 100 people on the floor. All we took from our home, just the stove. You should be able to cook something. This Hungarian, he was a-- oh, he was a big anti-Semite, who right away occupied our beautiful home, was the most modern furniture, with parquet, everything. Came next day, and he started to holler, what kind of nasty people are you? You took out the stove from this house? We had no other choice to just give him back the stove. So we couldn't already either. Chuck only dry food we had to eat because there was no chance to cook.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we were there approximately another two weeks or maybe a week. I can't tell you exactly. I don't remember. And one morning, it was approximately around 4:00 in the morning, usually the Germans and the Hungarians, everything was figured out [INAUDIBLE]. When you still sleeping, and you have no time to think, where you don't-- we are not concentrated how to help yourself. They woke you up suddenly with screaming, with dogs. There was Hungarian-- they call it [NON-ENGLISH]. They had like little feathers on their heads. Those were the most sadistic [NON-ENGLISH] were there in Hungary. If you saw on the streets, even before the ghetto, you saw such a guy, you were shaking. Somebody came to their hand, they beat him them up, his [INAUDIBLE]. He was never again in his life healthy. So they took the-- not the gun. How do you call the big--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rifle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rifle. They hold this rifle with the iron part.

speaker: interviewer
text: The bayonet.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Just the rifle. And with the wood part of the rifle, they started to scream and to knock and to beat everybody, 4:00 in the morning, out, out, out! Rush! Out! So we had already prepared for every four of us a sack on our back, a little clothing, a little food. And he wanted to knock on my head with this rifle, for them on the head, and to bring out blood was nothing. But I was bending down, and he knocked down my sack. So the sack opened up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So by the time already I went out to the street, the sack was empty. So I didn't have no more shirt, no more food. And they started to chase us from our street from the ghetto, to the factory-- a brick factory. I would say, roughly speaking, it took about two hours to walk there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Any Hungarian neighbor, if it was a growing up person or a young person, a stick in their hand and beating us. That's how we arrived to this. Hundreds of people had broken heads. There was a line of blood, the way you see a dividing line here on the road, you see a white or yellow dividing line, there was a red dividing line all over, until we arrived to the factory. The Hungarian were such a sadistic people. They made tent. If the tent would be at least so much to bend in to the bed, so at least we could bend-- they made such a low tent that a baby could go into this tent. So we didn't have to go into the tent, we had to sneak into the tent like a snake. Was terrible. No facilities. Once in a while a small piece of bread. For warm food, forget it. Every minute, this guy was beaten up, this one. We shouldn't even have time to think.

speaker: interviewee
text: After being there a week time, they felt that the people-- there will be something done. They say we have to give him something. Can you imagine a guilt feeling? And they took out a big, big bath tub. It was putting under the bath tub four or six legs made for the bath tub outside, made fire under the bath tub, threw in beads or those green whatever it is-- cheap stuff-- and they made a soup the public should have a little warm soup. So finally everybody was hoping, after a week time, being hungry, children were crying, screaming. So suddenly, the soup was ready, a little hot water, and people started to stay in the line with a little dish to go and get the soup. The first Jew, of course, most of them they didn't have beard. They cut them off the beard already. A father didn't recognize the son, and the son didn't recognize the father, and the wife didn't recognize the husband, because all their life they saw him with a beard, here without a beard. And he's staying there, one Jew, he still had a beard and staying with his little dish what has a holder. And they want to give him the first cup of soup. He was happy to bring it to his grandchild or even to his own baby. And the guy, this Hungarian officer, takes away from him the dish before he gets it. And he says you see this holder? Underneath there is dirt. Dirt. And if there is dirt, he didn't eat from his dish. The Jew would eat from the-- he beat him up so for this little dirt. The guy was full with blood. Slowly everybody left the line. Nobody want to have a soup anymore, because if you get [INAUDIBLE] the soup, to be killed for a soup, and then everybody left the line, not because they wanted to leave, because they was afraid to be killed like this particular Jew. The Hungarian said, you see? We made a soup for nothing. They are not hungry. And they took the whole soup and they threw it out, middle of the ghetto on the floor. The Germans, they didn't do never such a thing, but the Hungarian that how they started already in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: To tell you how bad was in this brick factory, two weeks to be a big factory was worse than to be one year in Auschwitz. So I can describe you what was it. Then, of course, the trains, they arrived. In the trains, they was putting in letters, probably a lot of Jews, they're forced to write these letters. And they writing in Jewish words. We arrived. It means that this train came already back from one of the shipments, who those first shipment arrived already to this labor camp. And the labor camp is in Hungary. They call it, they gave a name [NON-ENGLISH] And we have it fine. The children and older people are not working, only the men who are capable to work, they do labor work. In order to confuse the public that he's not going to Auschwitz, he's not going out from the Hungary, he's staying in [NON-ENGLISH] in the same town whatever it is in Hungary. And people believed it. Now, you will probably ask what you asked before this question, how come since 1939 when the Jews got taken already in Poland and other countries, and here we are in 1944, and we are so stupid. And we don't know where they're taking us.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarian, they used to fight. In 1940-- 14 until 1918, there was an Austrian Hungarian army. And they were officers, Jews, and they were you-- by Franz Joseph, the Jews had a lot of rights, and we had a good time for the Jews. So these-- the Hungarians, they was hoping, and it was believing that we, who they're fighting in Hungarian army, they do it for the Polish Jews, they do it for the other Jews, but we Hungarian. This wouldn't happen. You understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, when the Hungarian, they occupied in 1938, a lot of Jews still were in the Hungarian Army, officers and everything. So nobody believed it. And a lot of time, people came to tell stories what happened in Poland, and when you don't want to hear it, that story, you don't hear it. Happened once when Poland already, most of the Jews had taken to Poland, they came some of them who survived, run away, to Munkacs, nobody took them in the house because it was very dangerous. If they caught that Polish Jew in your house, next they took away your house, they took away your store, they took away your money, and you had to go to forced labor. So it was very dangerous. The way we organized it to help those Jews, we let them each time we left the older synagogues open. And they used to sleep in synagogues and once in a while we threw in food. I mean, they should survive. Once in a while my father, who was inviting such a Polish Jew to Friday night, but the supper when we had Friday night, and this Jew was by us, he was shivering because if one goy would see that a Jew-- a Polish came into us, next day we would be finished. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So they told us, the Polish people, what happened. So one day in the [INAUDIBLE] comes a Polish guy and tells us you know what happened in Poland? We says, what? He says, they took 200 Jews, they was put in a big room, and they were testing gas on them. So I couldn't believe such a thing on human being to test gas. So and you don't want to believe it, and that's how human nature is. One talk to the other. The guy's a little mixed up. He's stupid. If you want to test gas, why you have to test them on a Jew? You can test them on a dog.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, when we came to concentration camp, and we was chased with dogs that the Germans had like this, you mensch. It means they called the dog the human being and us they called the dogs. So he told the dog, you mensch-- actually dog-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- bite the dog. The dog was the Jew. So those days we didn't understand such a thing there is. So he probably knew more, and he wanted to explain us. We didn't want to believe it. So actually it was not a testing from gas, they gassed people, because a bullet for each Jew to give was maybe a penny or two.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took probably two cans in a gas chamber went, each time when they gassed people, 1,500 people went to one gas chamber. I was next to the gas chamber eight months. I knew the whole story what goes on. When I arrived to Auschwitz, the first half an hour I knew everything. Probably you could ask me why do I know and the rest they don't know? When they made the selection when we arrived, also we arrived to Auschwitz around 4-- 3:00, halfway dark, the minute they opened up, those gangsters, the doors from the trains, right away barking doors-- dogs. SS rush. [NON-ENGLISH] Fast. [NON-ENGLISH] Go. So you was mixed up. You didn't know what to do. Should you look for you sister? Should you look for the package? Should you look for your relatives? Should you look for your older man?

speaker: interviewee
text: First one, two, three, down from the train. And the Jewish-- Polish Jewish people who were there already many years, their work was to make it fast of the work, and they had also uniforms, these striped suits, and what they did, they tried-- they tried to save as much as they could by doing it, take away from the mother's hand, this baby, and give it right away over to an older person, to his mother, to his father.

speaker: interviewee
text: She should not, when it comes to the selection, because any minute started the selection. Here the man, here the woman, here you go right, here you go left. And we were screaming that they are-- they are gangsters, those Jewish, why? You took away my child. The mother, even if they would tell the mother that you go to the gas chamber, and this way you will be survive-- you will be survived, she wouldn't believe it. She would take the child and she would go to the gas chamber with the child. So that's what they did. They meant to help the people. But that's why it was such a hateness between those Hungarians and the Polish, because this Polish guy took away my child and didn't let me go wherever with my child. So right away started the selection. Mengele, who was the-- Dr. Mengele, the famous Josef Mengele, whom I met almost every week or second week, because he was studying us, because he himself had a pair of twins-- boys-- who are still today in Germany. They have a big factory. And now is a big sign that says Mengele Factory. It's a shame there is such a thing that they allow. But that's how the world is today. So he took-- he came, made the selection, right, left, right, left. Then he was looking for the twins. Of course, somebody told my uncle, told my father, listen Bentzion-- Bentzion was my father's name-- your children are twins, and I'm sure they're not going to go to hard labor, so report. Of course, my father was angry because he was that I should be with him. And my mother should be with my sister. But we didn't ask too much, it went fast. They took all the twins. And, of course, they went to the crematorium, and we went to a hospital department and there they started the study on us. As I arrived, let's say we arrived 5:00 in the morning, what I did to my barrack, and a Polish guy was there already many years. He told me Jewish, [NON-ENGLISH], come here, [NON-ENGLISH]. Come, I want to show you something. He say, you see on this side? You see they go in, young people? You will see in an hour, hour and a half, they will come out dressed up, shaved up, with striped suits.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see on this side, you will see nobody to come out. You will see only the oven will burn. And you will feel the smell how they're burning them up. So I knew already the first half an hour what's going on. And that was the reason I write it, I made a note for myself in my mind, not on paper, without a pencil. This was exactly three days before [NON-ENGLISH]. We counted Jews [INAUDIBLE] so you know there's three days before [NON-ENGLISH] they died, and that's how observing from the whole family who mostly did not survive. Three days before [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who exactly from your family was with you when you arrived at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the families were together. Grandfather and uncles and [NON-ENGLISH]. I have here family pictures I want to show it to you from those. Not even 5% came home. And even somebody with my-- I had from my father's brother's son who was in my age, and he became free, and as we became free, we all started to eat, and we became all sick. And he died there in Mauthausen. I saw many die. Those of us in my age.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported from Munkacs to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a train. With a train what usually they're transporting animals. But they didn't push in so many animals, cows or horses, in such a way as many as they put in human beings. Little windows. They put a few dishes, cans. This was the toilet. The terrible smell. The children were crying, hungry, thirsty. By the time we arrived before Auschwitz, there was a station, Krakow. This was a few kilometer. I don't know how many kilometers. I don't know the distance. And the-- the public outside by the station probably took water or whatever it is to continue the train. And the general public-- I don't know if-- how even the Germans they allowed it. They took a lot of water to fit some pipes and we was-- everybody was just a little, you know, in the water, water. Give us water.

speaker: interviewee
text: So even if they were anti-Semite, they was throwing on us the water with the pipes. And everybody was the happiest. If you would give in America somebody the whole Rockefeller Center, wouldn't be so happy as we got the little water on our face, because a few days already of the trains and hungry and thirsty. So I didn't take too long and then we arrive to Auschwitz. Do you know roughly how long the entire trip took?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say between two and three days. If you hold me responsible, I don't know. I just-- I can tell you roughly speaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that selection, how-- your father, you said, was the one who notified you to report as a twin or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- finish? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This Mengele, who made the selection, started to scream, zwillinge, zwillinge, ikrek, ikrek is Hungarian. Zwillinge and zwillinge is twins. Zwillinge is in German, Hungary is ikrek. And he already was prepared for all the three languages to notify the public that he needs zwillinge-- he needs twins, because he made experiment. The experiment was started already in 1944. And he started to make it on the German gypsies. Now, before I start to tell you about German gypsies, I have to explain it to you. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Hold up. We'll stop tape two here, and we'll pick this up with tape number three.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, fine.
